items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: How do decomposers return carbon to the atmosphere?
items.correct: They respire — breaking down dead organic matter and releasing CO2.
items.misconception: Decomposers release carbon by physically breaking matter into smaller pieces.
items.random_wrong: Decomposers turn dead matter into oxygen and water, with no carbon involved.
items.why: Students see decomposition as a physical breakdown rather than a metabolic respiration process happening in microbes and fungi.

items.q: Why is interspecific competition between similar species important?
items.correct: Two species cannot occupy exactly the same niche indefinitely — competition often leads to displacement or niche divergence.
items.misconception: Competition only happens between predators and their prey.
items.random_wrong: Competition means physically fighting and is rare in nature.
items.why: Students associate 'competition' with literal fighting, missing the ecological meaning of competing for shared resources.

items.q: Is energy created as it passes up a food chain?
items.correct: No — energy is transferred and much is lost (mainly as heat) at each level, so only about 10% passes on.
items.misconception: Energy is created at each trophic level, so top predators have the most.
items.random_wrong: Energy is destroyed entirely at the producer level.
items.why: Students assume predators gain energy, but energy only flows and dissipates, which is why food chains rarely have more than four or five links.

items.q: Are decomposers just animals that break dead matter into small pieces?
items.correct: No — decomposers (mainly bacteria and fungi) chemically break down dead matter by respiring it, recycling nutrients and releasing CO₂.
items.misconception: Decomposers only physically shred dead material into smaller bits.
items.random_wrong: Decomposers turn dead matter into oxygen with no carbon involved.
items.why: Students think decomposition is purely physical, but decomposers digest material chemically, returning nutrients to soil and carbon to the air.
It's Oak tree. “Rabbit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Temperature and light intensity are examples of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Abiotic factors. “Biotic factors” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A woodland community contains 4 species of tree, 12 species of bird, 6 species of mammal and 28 species of insect. How many species in total?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
